summary: Gruber reviews the 2022 10th-generation iPad, which adopts the modern all-screen design previously exclusive to the iPad Air and Pro. He methodically compares it against the iPad Air, noting the $150 price gap buys you a laminated P3 display, M1 chip with Stage Manager support, and Pencil 2 compatibility. He strongly recommends the Logitech Crayon over the Apple Pencil 1 for iPad 10 users, and finds the new Magic Keyboard Folio functional but overpriced at $250 without backlighting. The review also reveals that the Pencil 2 magnetic connector and front-facing camera physically cannot coexist on the same side of current iPads, explaining the camera placement differences. He concludes the iPad 10 is a solid product that brings the base iPad into the modern era, despite peripheral frustrations.
key_points: The iPad 10 fills a clear gap in the lineup between the $330 9th-gen iPad and the $600 iPad Air, with $150 steps between each tier making the lineup more logical, not more confusing
The Pencil 1 experience is markedly inferior to Pencil 2, and Gruber recommends the $70 Logitech Crayon as a better alternative for most iPad 10 users who don't need pressure sensitivity

The Magic Keyboard Folio at $250 without backlighting feels overpriced, especially since the $160 Logitech Combo Touch includes backlighting, and the keyboard has an inexplicably mismatched bracket key layout

The iPad 10's landscape front camera placement is clearly the future, but a source confirmed that the Pencil 2 magnetic mechanism and front camera physically cannot fit on the same side of current iPads
Using an iPad for work still feels like riding a bicycle uphill compared to a Mac, despite the iPad being excellent for focused long-form writing
money_quotes: A lot of people are now complaining that the iPad lineup is 'confusing'. I disagree.
Lamination puts the actual display closer to the surface of the glass — more like pixels on glass than pixels under glass.
Unless you need pressure sensitivity or dislike the feel of a carpenter's pencil in your hand, Logitech's new 2nd-gen Crayon is a clear winner compared to Apple's Pencil 1.
For $250 it feels wrong that it doesn't offer backlighting.
Clearly these two keys are siblings or peers, and ought to be the same width.
Going back to my Mac to catch up on everything else other than writing this article feels as liberating as taking off a pair of mittens while attempting to assemble a Lego kit.
Using any iPad for my work feels like riding a bicycle uphill; using a Mac feels like riding one downhill.
This mismatched key cap layout is so obviously wrong I just assumed it wouldn't happen again.
key_insight: The 2022 iPad successfully modernizes Apple's entry-level tablet, but its peripheral ecosystem — a dated Pencil, an overpriced keyboard, and incompatible accessories — undermines what should be a straightforward upgrade story.
